content.full_text:
FILING OBLIGATIONS OF
ALTERNATE MEMBERS

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322.5662        322.5660         322.5901        322.6441         322.6444

December 9, 1983


Anthony M. Summers, Deputy
Office of the Attorney General
110 West A Street, Ste. 700
San Diego, CA 92101

Re:  Your Request for Advice, Our
     Request No. A-83-239

Dear Mr. Summers:

     This letter is sent in reply to your memorandum which
detailed the filing of multiple assuming and leaving office
Statements of Economic Interests by Dwight Sanders and Brian
Sway, who act as alternates for California Coastal Commission
member Ken Cory.  This filing is based on your office's opinion
that Public Resources Code Section 30301.5 allows each Coastal
Commission member only one alternate.  You asked whether the
Commission could issue a ruling to make such multiple filings
unnecessary since the net result is simply more paper, not more
disclosure.  You indicated to me in a telephone conversation
that staff advice pursuant to Government Code Section 3114(b)
would be satisfactory.

     As you know, alternate members of governmental boards and
commissions are required to file Statements of Economic
Interests in the same manner as any other designated employee in
a conflict of interest code.  In the past, the Commission, in
its capacity as filing officer for state agencies and
multi-county local agencies, has required alternates to file
assuming office statements either when they first assume
decision-making authority (e.g., attend their first meeting) or
when they are initially appointed as alternates.  In any event,
both Dwight Sanders and Brian Sway have actually attended
meetings of the Coastal Commission as alternates for member Ken
Cory, and, as such, they are both required to file Statements of

Anthony M. Summers, Deputy
December 9, 1983
Page Two


Economic Interests. Even if you are correct that they lose that
status periodically under the Public Resources Code, as far as
we are concerned, they are both alternates within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act (Government Code Section 82019) until
they cease attending Coastal Commission meetings. They do not
need to file multiple assuming and leaving office statements;
instead each of them should file an annual statement.

We will place a copy of this letter and your memo in the
public Coastal Commission file of statements of Economic
Interests, and we suggest that you do the same for the agency's
public file.

Sincerely,

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel, Legal Division

DMF:km

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5562        322-5560        322-5901        322-6441        322-6444


October 24, 1983


Anthony M. Summers
Deputy Attorney General
110 W. A Street, Room 700
San Diego, CA 92101
                                                Re: A-83-239

Dear Mr. Summers:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Diane Fishburn, an attorney
in the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice
request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief, Legal Division

BAM:plh

State of California                    Department of Justic

Memorandum                                 110 West A Street, Suite 700
                                          San Diego, California 92101

To  :  Barbara Milman, Chief          Date : October 18, 1983
       Legal Division
       Fair Political Practices Commission    File No :
       1100 K Street
       Sacramento, California 95804          Telephone A155: ) 631-7590
                                                    (619) 237-7590


From : Office of the Attorney General - San Diego
       ANTHONY M. SUMMERS, Deputy Attorney General

Subject: Filing of FPPC Statements by Coast Commission Designees


This memorandum is being sent at the suggestion of Janis McLean
of your legal staff. We have been discussing a problem con-
cerning the filing of multiple taking and leaving office state-
ments by certain Coastal Commission members.

Public Resources Code section 30360(c) makes the chairperson of
the State Lands Commission a member of the California Coastal
Commission. Under section 30301.5 the chairperson is required
to appoint "a designee." Ron Cory, who chairs the Lands
Commission, designated Dwight Sandez pursuant to this section.
When Mr. Sanders was unable to attend a meeting, Mr. Cory named
Brian Sway to act instead of Mr. Sanders.

As the Deputy Attorney General providing legal advice to the
Coastal Commission, it was my conclusion that Mr. Cory could
have only one designee at any given time, although he could
change them as frequently as he wished. It was also my conclusion
that a designee could not appoint an alternate, since that would
create a situation where the non-voting members of the Commission
really had two alternates, while the voting members could only
have one each. I concluded that the non-voting members could
each have one designee, and the voting members could each have
one alternate.

Since Mr. Sanders and Mr. Sway each attended Coastal Commission
meetings on behalf of Mr. Cory, it was my determination that
every time one of them left and the other took over, the former
effectively resigned as a designee and the second assumed the
office. Consequently, it was necessary for each of them to file
the appropriate documents with your Commission dealing with
leaving and assuming office.

Needless to say, this has resulted in a good deal of paperwork
for the individuals involved and is undoubtedly flooding your
Commission with documents all containing the same information.

Barbara Milman
October 13, 1983
Page Two

I have been reluctant to follow any other course because failure
to file required documents could expose the individuals to
penalties under the Fair Political Practices Act. In the
absence of some change in the law, I will continue to advise
that Dr. Cory can have only one designee at a time. However, I
hope that your office can issue an appropriate ruling that will
make it unnecessary for the affected individuals to file state-
ments of economic interests if there is a change
in attendance at a Coastal Commission meeting.

Please keep me informed of any decision which you make in
connection with this matter. If you require further information,
I can be reached at ATSB 631-7594.

[illegible] M. SUTPHEN
Deputy Attorney General

cc:    Dwight Sanders
      Brian Sway
      R. Gregory Taylor
      Steven H. Kaufmann

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

(916) 322-5662          322-5660           322-5901           322-6441              322-6444


December 9, 1983


Anthony M. Summers, Deputy
Office of the Attorney General
110 West A Street, Ste. 700
San Diego, CA 92101

                                        Re: Your Request for Advice, Our
                                            Request No. A-83-239

Dear Mr. Summers:

detailed the filing of multiple assuming and leaving office

Commission could issue a ruling to make such multiple filings

meetings of the Coastal Commission as alternates for member Ken
Cory, and, as such, they are both required to file Statements of

Anthony M. Summers, Deputy
December 9, 1983
Page Two


Economic Interests.  Even if you are correct that they lose that
status periodically under the Public Resources Code, as far as
we are concerned, they are both alternates within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act (Government Code Section 82019) until
they cease attending Coastal Commission meetings.  They do not
need to file multiple assuming and leaving office statements;
instead each of them should file an annual statement.

We will place a copy of this letter and your memo in the
public Coastal Commission file of Statements of Economic
Interests, and we suggest that you do the same for the agency's
public file.

Sincerely,

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel, Legal Division

State of California

To    :  Barbara Milman, Chief

Date    :  October 18, 1983

File No.:

                (619)  237-7590

From  :  Office of the Attorney General - San Diego
         ANTHONY M. SUMMERS, Deputy Attorney General

October 18, 1983
Page Two


I have been reluctant to follow any other course because failure

Please keep me informed of any decision which you make in
connection with this matter. If you require further information,
I can be reached at ATSS 631-7590.


[signature]
ANTHONY M. SUMMERS
Deputy Attorney General
